content:
You are the Accumulator. Given one paper (markdown + optionally a cloned
GitHub repo), do TWO things:
  1. Assign the paper to ONE of the 7 ML domains below.
  2. Extract 1–3 distinct main contributions as seeds.

Each seed captures (a) the underlying problem the paper addresses, and
(b) the method that solves it, including what is special / novel about
the approach. Avoid splitting one idea into multiple sub-seeds; prefer
fewer, higher-quality seeds.

## Domains (pick exactly one)
- LLM_NLP    : language models, NLP, text-only agents
- MULTIMODAL : cross-modality (text+image, text+audio, etc.).
               Vision-Language Models (VLM, e.g., CLIP / LLaVA / GPT-4V)
               belong HERE, not under CV.
- CV         : visual perception (classification, detection, segmentation,
               video understanding) AND image/video generation
               (diffusion, GAN, VAE) when the contribution is the
               generative mechanism on visual data.
- RL         : reinforcement learning algorithms, policies, environments;
               RLHF when the contribution is the RL algorithm itself.
- IR_REC     : information retrieval, recommender systems, search.
- SPEECH     : speech / audio (TTS, ASR, audio language models).
- ROBOTICS   : embodied agents, manipulation, planning in physical world.
               If a paper proposes an embodied policy, prefer ROBOTICS
               over RL.

## Workspace contract
Paper directory:    /work/1801.01750
paper.md            Pre-converted markdown of the PDF.
repo/               Will exist if you `git clone` it. Otherwise unavailable.

## Tools
- Read       : view paper.md and any file under the paper directory
- Grep       : search inside paper.md or the cloned repo
- Bash       : `git clone <url> /work/1801.01750/repo` (only if you've found a
               *clearly official* GitHub URL in the paper);
               also general filesystem exploration: tree / ls / find / wc

## Output (terminate the run)
When you have finished, stop calling tools and reply with a SINGLE JSON
object inside a ```json fenced block. No extra prose:
  ```json
  {"title": "...", "domain": "<one of 7>", "seeds": [{"problem": "...", "method": "..."}, ...]}
  ```
- title  = the paper's actual title (NOT a venue label or page header)
- domain = exactly one of LLM_NLP / MULTIMODAL / CV / RL / IR_REC / SPEECH / ROBOTICS
- seeds  = 1–3 entries, or empty list if extraction failed

## Default flow
1. Read paper.md end-to-end. Identify the paper's true title (often the
   first H1, but PDF→md sometimes places venue labels / preprint headers
   above it — pick the actual title, not the noise). For longer papers
   a single Read may not cover everything; continue with Read(offset=...)
   until you actually understand the methodology and experiments
   sections, not just the abstract and introduction.
2. Decide the domain from the 7 above. Use the tie-breakers in the list
   when a paper sits between two.
3. Look for an official GitHub URL (abstract, footnote 1, "Code:" line, etc.).
   If you find one and it is unambiguously the paper's repo, clone it.
   If you are unsure, do NOT clone — proceed paper-only.
4. (If repo cloned) Use Bash / Grep to locate where the main contribution
   is implemented. Read enough to confirm what's special.
5. Draft 1–3 candidate seeds in your reasoning. Each seed:
     - problem: 1–2 sentences. The challenge the paper addresses.
     - method: 3–5 sentences. The solution AND what is special about it.
6. BEFORE emitting the final JSON, verify each candidate seed against
   the paper. For each seed:
     - Identify 2-3 specific claims your `method` text makes
       (mechanism name, key design choice, quantitative finding).
     - Use Grep on paper.md to locate where each claim is supported,
       and Read(offset=...) to inspect the surrounding context if the
       Grep snippet is insufficient. (Use repo Grep/Read if cloned.)
     - Revise the seed text to match the paper. If a claim cannot be
       grounded after re-reading, REMOVE that claim or drop the seed
       entirely.
   This step is MANDATORY: do not skip it because a seed feels right.
7. Stop calling tools and emit the final JSON object described under
   "## Output". This terminates the run.

Budget: 100 tool calls. Plan first, then act.

## Paper
Paper directory: /work/1801.01750

Begin by reading paper.md.

name: Read
content:
1	## Nonparametric Stochastic Contextual Bandits
     2	
     3	Melody Y. Guan ∗
     4	
     5	Stanford University 450 Serra Mall Stanford, California 94305
     6	
     7	mguan@stanford.edu
     8	
     9	## Abstract
    10	
    11	We analyze the K -armed bandit problem where the reward for each arm is a noisy realization based on an observed context under mild nonparametric assumptions. We attain tight results for top-arm identification and a sublinear regret of ˜ O ( T 1+ D 2+ D ) , where D is the context dimension, for a modified UCB algorithm that is simple to implement ( k NN-UCB). We then give global intrinsic dimension dependent and ambient dimension independent regret bounds. We also discuss recovering topological structures within the context space based on expected bandit performance and provide an extension to infinite-armed contextual bandits. Finally, we experimentally show the improvement of our algorithm over existing multi-armed bandit approaches for both simulated tasks and MNIST image classification.
    12	
    13	## Introduction
    14	
    15	Multi-armed bandits (MABs) are an important sequential optimization problem introduced by Robbins (1985). These models have extensively been used in a wide variety of fields related to statistics and machine learning.
    16	
    17	The classical MAB consists of K arms where at each point in time the learner can sample (or pull) one of them and observe a reward. Then various objectives can be established, such as finding the best arm (Top-Arm Identification) or minimizing some regret over time.
    18	
    19	For contextual bandits (also referred to as bandits with side information or covariates), the learner has access to a context on which the payoffs depend. Then, based on the observations, we aim to determine the best policy (or contextto-arm mapping) and to optimize some notion of regret.
    20	
    21	Most approaches to stochastic contextual bandits make strong assumptions on the payoffs. A popular approach models the mean reward for each arm as being linear in the context space (Chu et al. 2011; Li et al. 2010). However, this is rarely the case in real data. In this paper, we take a more general approach and allow the reward functions to be non-linear and of arbitrary shape.
    22	
    23	Using recent developments in nonparametric statistics (Jiang 2017b), we show that with simple and easily implementable techniques, we can construct bandit algorithms
    24	
    25	∗ Equal Contribution.
    26	
    27	Copyright c © 2018, Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (www.aaai.org). All rights reserved.
    28	
    29	## Heinrich Jiang ∗
    30	
    31	Google
    32	
    33	1600 Amphitheatre Pwky Mountain View, California 94043 heinrich.jiang@gmail.com
    34	
    35	which can learn over the entire context space with strong guarantees, despite the difficulty that arises with allowing a wide variety of reward functions. While this is not the first work which blends nonparametric statistics with bandits, we are the first to show simple and practical methods while still maintaining strong theoretical guarantees.
    36	
    37	We reanalyze the uniform and upper confidence bound sampling strategies and demonstrate what nonparametric approaches can offer to contextual bandit learning. No other technique can adapt to the inherently difficult and complex real world reward functions while allowing such a strong theoretical understanding of the underlying algorithms.
    38	
    39	While nonparametric models are powerful in their ability to learn arbitrary functions free of distributional assumptions, a major weakness is the curse of dimensionality. In order to have any theoretical guarantees, they require an exponential-in-dimension number of samples. However, when the data lies on an unknown low-dimensional structure such as a manifold, we show that our algorithms can converge as if the data was on a lower dimension and not in the potentially much large ambient dimension. Another striking fact is that no preprocessing of the data is required. This is of practical importance because modern data has increasingly more features but the underlying degrees of freedom often remain small.
    40	
    41	We then discuss recovering geometric structures in the context space based on bandit performance. Specifically, we recover the connected components of the context space in which a particular bandit is the top-arm. Although learning a context-to-arm mapping gives us the estimated top-arm at each point in the context space, this alone does not tell the space's topological structure, such as the number and shapes of connected components. We recover these structures with uniform consistency guarantees with mild assumptions, where the shapes and relative positions of the components can be arbitrary and the number of such components is recovered automatically.
    42	
    43	We then provide an extension to infinite-armed bandits and conclude with empirical results from simulations and image classification on the MNIST dataset.
    44	
    45	## Setup
    46	
    47	Suppose there are K bandit arms indexed in [ K ] . At each time-step t , the learner observes a context x t ∈ R D
    48	
    49	where x t is drawn i.i.d. from a context density p X with compact support X bounded below away from zero (e.g. inf x ∈X p X ( x ) ≥ p 0 for some p 0 ≥ 0 ). Then the learner chooses an arm I t ∈ [ K ] and observes reward
    50	
    51	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
    52	
    53	where ξ t is drawn according to white noise random variable ξ and f i : X → R is the i -th arm's mean reward. We make the following assumptions.
    54	
    55	Assumption 1. (Lipschitz Mean Reward) There exists L such that | f i ( x ) -f i ( x ′ ) | ≤ L | x -x ′ | for all x, x ′ ∈ X and i ∈ [ K ] .
    56	
    57	Assumption 2. (Sub-Gaussian White noise) ξ satisfies E [ ξ ] = 0 and is sub-Gaussian with parameter σ 2 (i.e. E [exp( λξ )] ≤ exp( σ 2 λ 2 / 2) for all λ ∈ R ).
    58	
    59	We require the finite-sample strong uniform consistency result (Theorem 1) for k -NN regression defined as fellows: Definition 1 ( k -NN) . Let the k -NN radius of x ∈ X be r k ( x ) := inf { r : | B ( x, r ) ∩ X | ≥ k } where B ( x, r ) := { x ∈ X : | x -x ′ | ≤ r } and the k -NN set of x ∈ X be N k ( x ) := X ∩ B ( x, r k ( x )) . Then for x ∈ X ,

    66	
    67	It will be implicitly understood from here on that ̂ f i denotes the k -NN regression estimate of f i under the settings of Theorem 1.
    68	
    69	## Top-Arm Identification
    70	
    71	## Algorithm 1 Uniform Sampling
    72	
    73	- 1: Parameters: T , total number of time steps.
    74	- 2: For each arm i of the K arms:
    75	- 4: Pull arm I t := i .
    76	- 3: For each time step t ∈ [ ( i -1) T K +1 , iT K ] :

    78	
    79	Definition 2. ( glyph[epsilon1] -optimal arm) Arm i is be glyph[epsilon1] -optimal at context x ∈ X if max j ∈ [ K ] f j ( x ) -f i ( x ) ≤ glyph[epsilon1] .
    80	
    81	Following we show a uniform (over context) result about glyph[epsilon1] -optimal arm recovery:
    82	
    83	Theorem 2. ( glyph[epsilon1] -optimal arm recovery) Let δ > 0 . For Algorithm 1, with probability at least 1 -δ/K , if

    88	
    89	Remark 1. This result shows that with ˜ O ( glyph[epsilon1] -(2+ D ) ) samples, we can determine an glyph[epsilon1] -approximate best arm. Known lower bounds in nonparametric regression stipulate that we need Ω( glyph[epsilon1] -(2+ D ) ) to identify differences between functions of size glyph[epsilon1] so our result matches lower bounds up to logarithmic factors.
    90	
    91	Proof. By Theorem 1, it follows that based on the choice of T , each arm has at least enough time such that sup x ∈X | ̂ f i ( x ) -f i ( x ) | ≤ glyph[epsilon1]/ 2 . Thus, we have ∀ x ∈ X , defining π ( x ) = max j ∈ [ K ] f j ( x ) ,

    94	
    95	## Regret Analysis For UCB Strategy
    96	
    97	Define T i ( t ) to be the number of times arm i was pulled by time t .
    98	
    99	## Algorithm 2 Upper Confidence Bound (UCB)
   100	
   101	- 1: Parameters: M 0 , M 1 , δ , T .
   102	- 2: Define σ ( n ) = M 1 √ log n (log( nK/δ )) · n -1 / (2+ D ) .
   103	- 3: Pull each of the K arms M 0 times.
   104	- 4: For each round t = KM 0 , KM 0 +1 , . . . , T :

   106	
   107	We use the following notion of regret.
   108	
   109	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
   110	
   111	Remark 2. Note that this notion of regret is different from those studied in classical MABs as well as other works in nonparametric contextual bandits. Usually the expected form E [ R T ] is bounded. Here, our regret analysis is not under this expectation and hence is a stronger notion of regret.
   112	
   113	Theorem 3. Let δ > 0 . Suppose that M 0 ≥ N 0 and M 1 > C in Algorithm 2. Then we have that with probability at least 1 -δ ,

   118	
   119	Proof. Denote ̂ f i,T i ( t ) to be the k -NN regression estimate of f i at time t . Letting C 0 = KM 0 max i || f i || ∞ , we have by Theorem 1

   122	
   123	The first inequality holds because the confidence bound of a sub-optimal arm must be higher than that of the optimal at x t in order for that arm to be chosen and the regret at that time-step is bounded by the confidence bound. The second inequality holds because of the following simple combinatorial argument. Each time a suboptimal arm is chosen, its count increments, or otherwise there is no regret incurred.
   124	
   125	## Contextual Bandits on Manifolds
   126	
   127	Assumption 3. (Manifold Assumption) p X and the family of f i are supported on M , where:
   128	
   129	- M is a d -dimensional smooth compact Riemannian manifold without boundary embedded in compact subset X ⊆ R D .
   130	- The volume of M is bounded above by a constant.
   131	- M has condition number 1 /τ , which controls the curvature and prevents self-intersection.
   132

   148

   150

   152	
   153	## Topological Analysis
   154	
   155	In this section, we discuss how topological features about the bandit arms can be recovered. This is similar to recovering the Hartigan notion of clusters as level-sets of the density functions from a finite sample (Chaudhuri and Dasgupta 2010; Jiang 2017a), but here, we find similar structures in the reward functions based on noisy observations of them. We give procedures which can estimate with consistency guarantees the following structure: maximal connected regions in X where a particular arm is the top-arm.
   156	
   157	From the uniform sampling strategy earlier, we obtained estimated policy ˆ π which is δ -optimal uniformly in X with high probability. Although this is already powerful in giving us the mapping between context space and the corresponding top-arm, it does not immediately tell us the topological features of this mapping. In this subsection, we discuss how to recover the connected components of { x ∈ X : r i ( x ) = max j ∈ [ K ] r j ( x ) } , the region where arm i is the top-arm.
   158	
   159	We give the following simple procedure.
   160	
   161	Algorithm 3 Recovering Regions where i -th arm is top arm.
   162	
   163	- 1: Given: Bandit arm i and R > 0 .
   164	- 2: Pull each of the K arms T/K times.
   165	- 3: Let G be the graph with vertices { x t : t ∈ [ T ] , ̂ f i ( x t ) = max j ∈ [ K ] ̂ f j ( x t ) } and edges between vertices whose euclidean distance is at most R .
   166	- 4: return The connected components of G .
   167	
   168	We now give a consistency result for Algorithm 3.
   169	
   170	First, we require the following regularity assumption, which ensures that there are no full-dimensional regions where the top-arm is not unique. This ensures that it is possible to unambiguously recover the regions where a particular arm is top.
   171	
   172	Assumption 4. The region in X where the top-arm is not unique has measure 0 , and for each arm i , the region X i where it is unique can be partitioned into full-dimensional connected components.
   173	
   174	Our rates will be in terms of the Hausdorff distance.

   207	
   208	Next, we show that for each connected component C p , there exists ̂ C q for some q ∈ [ ̂ l ] such that d H ( ̂ C q , C p ) → 0 . It suffices to show that for each r > 0 , we have that for n sufficiently large, d H ( ̂ C q , C p ) < r . There are thus two directions to show, that ̂ C p ⊆ C q ⊕ r and C q ⊆ ̂ C p ⊕ r . To show the first, define

   223	
   224	All that remains is to show that such points appear in in the same connected component in the graph computed by Algorithm 3. This follows from uniform concentration bounds on balls (e.g. Chaudhuri and Dasgupta (2010)).
   225	
   226	## Infinite-Armed Bandits
   227	
   228	In this section, we consider the setting where the action space A is no longer a finite set of bandits, but a compact subset of R D ′ for some D ′ > 0 .
   229	
   230	We given analogous results for the uniform sampling toparm identification and regret bounds for UCB-type strategy.
   231	
   232	Definition 4. (Mean Reward function)

   236	where f ( x, a ) is the expected reward of action a ∈ A at context x ∈ X .
   237	
   238	Assumption 5. (Lipschitz Reward) There exists L > 0 such that for all x, x ′ ∈ X and a, a ′ ∈ A , | f ( x, a ) -f ( x ′ , a ′ ) | ≤ L | ( x, a ) -( x ′ , a ′ ) | , where ( x, a ) represents the ( D + D ′ ) -dimensional concatenation of x and a .
   239	
   240	Then at each time t , the learner chooses arm a t ∈ A and observes context x t ∈ X and a stochastic reward

   243	
   244	where ξ 1 , ... are i.i.d. white noise with mean 0 and variance σ 2 .
   245	
   246	## Algorithm 4 Infinite-Armed Uniform Sampling
   247	
   248	- 1: Parameters: T , total number of time steps.
   249	- 2: For t = 1 , ..., T :
   250	- 3: Pull I t , sampled uniformly from A .
   251	- 4: Observe context x t and reward R t .
   252	- 5: Define ˆ f to be the k -NN regression estimate from samples ( a 1 , R 1 ) , ..., ( a T , R T ) with setting k = glyph[floorleft] n 2 / (2+ D + D ′ ) glyph[floorright] .
   253	
   254	Definition 5. ( glyph[epsilon1] -optimal arm) Define arm a ∈ A to be glyph[epsilon1] -optimal at context x ∈ X if sup a ′ ∈A f ( x, a ′ ) -f ( x, a ) ≤ glyph[epsilon1] .
   255	
   256	Following is a uniform (over context and action space) result about glyph[epsilon1] -optimal arm recovery:
   257	
   258	Theorem 8. ( glyph[epsilon1] -optimal arm recovery) There exists constant ˜ C 1 , ˜ C 2 such that the following holds. Let δ > 0 . For Algorithm 4, with probability at least 1 -δ , we have that for
   259

   263	
   264	Proof. By Theorem 1, it follows that based on the choice of T , there is enough time spent on pulling each arm such that sup a ∈A ,x ∈X | ˆ f ( x, a ) -f ( x, a ) | ≤ glyph[epsilon1]/ 2 . Thus, we have ∀ x ∈ X , defining π ( x ) = argmax a ∈A f ( x, a ) ,
   265	
   266	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
   267	
   268	as desired.
   269	
   270	Algorithm 5 Infinite-Armed Upper Confidence Bound (UCB)
   271	
   272	- 1: Parameters: M , M 1 , T
   273	- 2: Define σ ( n ) = M 1 n -1 / (2+ D + D ′ ) .
   274	- 3: For t = 1 , ..., M :
   275	- 4: Sample a t uniformly from A .
   276	- 5: Observe context x t and reward R t .
   277	- 6: For t = M +1 , ..., T :
   278	- 7: Choose I t := argmax a ∈A ̂ f ( x t , a ) + σ ( t ) .
   279	
   280	Finally, using the notion of regret
   281	
   282	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
   283	
   284	we give the following result. The proof idea is similar to that of Theorem 3 and is omitted here.
   285	
   286	Theorem 9. There exists ˜ C 1 and ˜ C 2 such that the following holds. Let δ > 0 . Suppose that M and M 1 are chosen sufficiently large in Algorithm 5 depending on f and σ . Then we have that with probability at least 1 -δ ,
   287	
   288	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
   289	
   290	Remark 5. This shows a sub-linear regret of ˜ O ( T 1+ D + D ′ 2+ D + D ′ ) .
   291	
   292	## Related Works
   293	
   294	Canonical works for the standard bandit problem are Lai and Robbins (1985); Berry and Fristedt (1985); Gittins, Glazebrook, and Weber (2011); Auer et al. (2002); Cesa-Bianchi and Lugosi (2006); Bubeck and Cesa-Bianchi (2012).
   295	
   296	Work in contextual bandits can be roughly classified into adversarial and stochastic approaches. Much of the former, initiated by Auer et al. (2002), assumes that there is an adversarial game between nature and the learner where, based on a context seen by both players, nature generates rewards for each arm at the same time the learner chooses an arm. Solutions typically involve game theoretical methods. In the stochastic approach, one assumes that the rewards for the arms are generated by a context-dependent distribution.
   297	
   298	Approaches to modeling the arm rewards as a function of context are most commonly parametric. One of the most popular is that of linear payoffs, studied under a minimax framework (Goldenshluger and Zeevi 2009; 2013), with UCB-type algorithms (Chu et al. 2011; Li et al. 2010; Auer et al. 2002), or with Thompson sampling (Agrawal and Goyal 2013).
   299	
   300	However, it is often the case that the dependency between the payoffs and the contexts are complex and therefore difficult to capture with models such as linear payoffs, many of which requiring strong assumptions on the data. To alleviate this, we can go beyond parametric modeling and blend nonparametric statistics with contextual bandits. Despite the advantage of learning much more general context-payoff dependencies, this line of work has received far less attention.
   301	
   302	To the best of our knowledge, the first such work appeared in Yang and Zhu (2002), who used histogram, k -NN, and kernel methods and showed asymptotic convergence rates. Rigollet and Zeevi (2010); Perchet and Rigollet (2013) then combined histogram-type binning techniques in nonparametric statistics to obtain strong regret guarantees for contextual bandits with optimality guarantees.
   303	
   304	Lu, P´ al, and P´ al (2010) study an interesting setting where the reward depends on a Lipschitz measure which is jointly in the context and the action space. They provide upper and lower regret bounds based on a covering argument and give results in terms of the packing dimension. This is highly related to the infinite-armed bandit setting in the present work; we provide similar regret guarantees but with a simple and practical procedure.
   305	
   306	More recently, Qian and Yang (2016b); Qian and Yang (2016a) use the strong uniform consistency properties of kernel smoothing regression to establish regret guarantees.
   307	
   308	Langford and Zhang (2008); Dudik et al. (2011) alternatively impose neither linear nor smoothness assumptions on the mean reward function. The former propose a modification of an glyph[epsilon1] -greedy policy and showed that expected regret converges to 0 while the latter considers a finite class of policies.
   309	
   310	In this paper, using recent finite-sample results about k -NN regression established in Jiang (2017b), we show that using the simple k -NN regression is an effective alternative approach. Moreover, unlike many other nonparametric techniques, k -NN adapts to a lower intrinsic dimension (Kpotufe 2011) and thus we show that our regret bounds can adapt to a lower intrinsic dimension automatically and perform as if we were operating in that lower dimensional space.
   311	
   312	## Experiments
   313	
   314	## Simulations
   315	
   316	We consider three two-arm bandit scenarios in the twodimensional unit square, where p X is uniform. We set arm i ∈ { 1 , 2 } to be top in region R i respectively. Figure 1 illustrates the regions for the different scenarios.
   317	
   318	- Scenario 1 (Quintic Function): We define two regions above and below a quintic function:
   319	- Scenario 2 (Smiley): We use two circles and a semicircle to demarcate the regions in a 'smiley face' pattern.
   320	- Scenario 3 (Bullseye): We define the regions using the alternating regions of four concentric circles centered in the support.
   321	
   322	The true reward functions of the two arms are as follows.
   323	
   324	glyph[negationslash]

   327	
   328	The learner observes the rewards with white noise random variable ξ ∼ N ( µ = 0 , σ = 0 . 5) .
   329	
   330	We compare the performance of k -NN regression (nonparametric) and Ridge regression at top-arm identification and regret minimization in the three scenarios. Mirroring our theoretical discussion, we use uniform sampling for top-arm identification and UCB strategy for regret analysis. Note that Ridge regression with UCB is the LinUCB algorithm.

   355	
   356	Qualitative Analysis We first qualitatively show that k -NN regression can successfully model the bandits whereas the linear method cannot. The difficulty of the task is illustrated by Figure 2, which plots 10k uniformly sampled samples from each scenario with a colormap. We can see that a human would have a hard time recovering the regions where each arm is top due to the randomness in the observed rewards. This randomness is considerable as we set σ = 0 . 5 to be the same as | f i ( x ∈ R i ) -f i ( x / ∈ R i ) | .
   357	
   358	We fix the number of training samples N to 10k and the number of nearest neighbors to k = 25 . We evaluate on 10k random test samples. Figure 3 shows that k -NN regression does an excellent job of reproducing the region boundaries. Ridge regression does a poor job in the Quintic Function case, making a linear approximation to the quintic curve, and completely fails in the Smiley and Bullseye Cases, simply choosing the arm whose top-arm region is larger.
   359	
   360	Quantitative Analysis We report numerical results and optimal hyperparameters in Table 1. We tuned other hyperparameters using grid search on a validation set of size 1k using grid search and we evaluate performance of our models on a test set of size 1k. We use the UCB strategy in Auer et al. (2002) (a simplified version of UCB by Agrawal and Goyal (2013)). We found that a confidence level of 0 . 1 worked well for all settings. We see that k -NN significantly outperforms Ridge regression for both top-arm identification and regret minimization in all three scenarios (Table 1).
   361	
   362	## Image Classification Experiments
   363	
   364	We extend our experiments to image classification of the canonical MNIST dataset, which consists of 60k training images and 10k test images of isolated, normalized, handwritten digits. The task is to classify each 28 × 28 image into one of ten classes. We reframe this as a contextual MAB problem by treating the classes as arms and the images as the contexts. Note that for every context, the payoff of all arms are known: 1 if the class is the true label and 0 otherwise. We compare k -NN and Ridge regressions at regret minimization using the UCB strategy. As before we use the UCB strategy in Auer et al. (2002) and fix the confidence level to 0.1. We do not employ any data augmentation.
   365	
   366	We obtain test regret of 17.5% from LinUCB with α = 5 , where α is the coefficient of L2 regularization, and significantly lower test regret of 5.8% from 4-NNUCB. Figure
   367	
   368	4 shows that k -NN regression maintains lower regret than Ridge regression over a range of values of k and α . We note that Ridge regression working well for relatively large values of α itself suggests that it is a poor model for the task.
   369	
   370	Figure 4: Test results on regret minimization for MNIST image classification over varied values of α (for LinUCB) and k (for k -NNUCB). The nonparametric approach achieves significantly lower regret over a range of hyperparameters.
   371	
   372	<!-- image -->
   373	
   374	## Conclusion
   375	
   376	For the multi-armed bandit setting, we use nonparametric regression to attain tight results for top-arm identification and a sublinear regret of ˜ O ( T 1+ D 2+ D ) , where D is the dimension of the context. We also show that if the underlying context space has a lower intrinsic dimension d < D , then our algorithm automatically adapts to the lower dimension and attains a faster rate of ˜ O ( T 1+ d 2+ d ) . We also provide a procedure for recovering the maximal connected regions in a support where a particular arm is the top-arm and provide a consistency analysis. We then give a natural extension to infinitearmed contextual bandits. Our simulations confirm that our method is able to learn in the contextual setting with arbitrary decision boundaries, even in the presence of significant noise, and our experiments on classification of MNIST images demonstrate superior performance of our method over LinUCB on a real world task.
